coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots enable efficient photothermal conversion for direct absorption solar collectors
coredata.prism:publicationName: Solar Energy Materials and Solar Cells
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Yuxin
Dong, Chuanshuai
Peng, Chaohua
Zhang, Tao
Zhang, Lizhi
coredata.dc:description: Direct absorption solar collector (DASC) using nanofluids is an efficient way to utilize solar energy. The heat collection efficiency in DASC is constrained by the stability and optical absorption capacity of nanofluids. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) nanofluids are used in DASC due to the excellent dispersion stability. However, the optical absorption range of current CQDs is primarily confined to the ultraviolet region, resulting in the visible and near-infrared regions of sunlight being underutilized. To solve this problem, this study synthesized novel N-doped carbon quantum dots (FPNCQDs) using ammonium bifluoride (NH4F) and perylene derivatives. The addition of NH4F and perylene derivatives during synthesis increased the sp2 conjugated structure and the content of graphite N in FPNCQDs. The doping of N, especially graphite N, significantly reduces the band gap by injecting excess electrons into the unoccupied π∗ orbitals. The temperature of FPNCQDs/EG nanofluids increased from 24 °C to 65.7 °C within 60 min, demonstrating excellent photothermal conversion performance. What's more, the FPNCQDs/EG nanofluids achieved high stability with nearly consistent transmittance over a 14-day storage test. Finally, a theoretical model for the photothermal conversion process of the nanofluids was developed to investigate the effect to carbon quantum dots on the solar collection performance. The simulation results indicated that the FPNCQDs/EG nanofluids at 10 ppm demonstrated the highest solar collection efficiency considering both photothermal conversion and heat loss from the surface. The novel FPNCQDs/EG nanofluid will be a promising photothermal fluids in direct absorption solar collector.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Direct absorption solar collector
Nanofluids
Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots
Photothermal conversion

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the experimental setup for DASC.

(a) TEM image and size distribution of FPNCQDs; (b) HRTEM image and magnified views for the lattice fringes of FPNCQDs; (c) TEM image and size distribution of NCQDs; (d) HRTEM image and magnified views for the lattice fringes of NCQDs.
(a) FTIR patterns of the PTCDA, FPNCQDs and NCQDs; (b) Raman spectra of the FPNCQDs and NCQDs.
(a) XPS spectra of FPNCQDs and NCQDs. High resolution XPS spectra in (b) C 1s region, (c) N 1s region, and (d) O 1s region for FPNCQDs and NCQDs, respectively.
(a) The transmittance and (b) extinction coefficient of EG, FPNCQDs/EG and NCQDs/EG nanofluids.
The spectral irradiance of EG, FPNCQDs/EG, and NCQDs/EG nanofluids (100 ppm).
Band gap energy of FPNCQDs and NCQDs.
(a) Transmittance and (b) Spectral irradiance of FPNCQDs/EG nanofluids with different concentrations.

Photos of (a) FPNCQDs/EG and (b) NCQDs/EG nanofluids at different concentrations before and after 14 days of storage. The transmittance of (c) FPNCQDs/EG nanofluids and (d) NCQDs/EG nanofluids before (solid lines) and after 14 days (dotted lines).
Cycled heating and cooling performance of FPNCQDs/EG nanofluids (20 ppm). (H1 and H2 represent the heights of two thermocouples located 0.6 cm and 1.3 cm below the upper surface of the nanofluids.)

Temperature variation with irradiation time for FPNCQDs/EG nanofluids (20 ppm), NCQDs/EG nanofluids (20 ppm), and EG, respectively. (H1 and H2 represent the heights of two thermocouples located at 0.6 cm and 1.3 cm below the upper surface of the nanofluids.)
Average temperature variation with irradiation time of FPNCQDs/EG nanofluids with different concentrations.
Comparison between the experimental and simulated values.
Temperature distributions of the DASC with different concentrations. (a) EG; (b) 10 ppm; (c) 50 ppm; (d) 100 ppm.
Solar collection efficiency of the DASC at different concentrations.
Mesh-independence test for the DASC simulation.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots enable efficient photothermal conversion for direct absorption solar collectors

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Key Laboratory of Enhanced Heat Transfer and Energy Conservation of Education Ministry, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, South China University of Technology, Guangzhou, 510640, China.n

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Enhanced Heat Transfer and Energy Conservation of Education Ministry
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Direct absorption solar collector (DASC) using nanofluids is an efficient way to utilize solar energy. The heat collection efficiency in DASC is constrained by the stability and optical absorption capacity of nanofluids. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) nanofluids are used in DASC due to the excellent dispersion stability. However, the optical absorption range of current CQDs is primarily confined to the ultraviolet region, resulting in the visible and near-infrared regions of sunlight being underutilized. To solve this problem, this study synthesized novel N-doped carbon quantum dots (FPNCQDs) using ammonium bifluoride (NH4F) and perylene derivatives. The addition of NH4F and perylene derivatives during synthesis increased the sp2 conjugated structure and the content of graphite N in FPNCQDs. The doping of N, especially graphite N, significantly reduces the band gap by injecting excess electrons into the unoccupied π∗ orbitals. The temperature of FPNCQDs/EG nanofluids increased from 24 °C to 65.7 °C within 60 min, demonstrating excellent photothermal conversion performance. What's more, the FPNCQDs/EG nanofluids achieved high stability with nearly consistent transmittance over a 14-day storage test. Finally, a theoretical model for the photothermal conversion process of the nanofluids was developed to investigate the effect to carbon quantum dots on the solar collection performance. The simulation results indicated that the FPNCQDs/EG nanofluids at 10 ppm demonstrated the highest solar collection efficiency considering both photothermal conversion and heat loss from the surface. The novel FPNCQDs/EG nanofluid will be a promising photothermal fluids in direct absorption solar collector.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:def-description.ce:para: solar collector area of the DASC, m2
radiative intensity at a given position following the Ω direction, W/(m2·sr)
absorbed solar irradiance of nanofluids, W/(m2·nm)
AM 1.5 solar irradiance spectrum in light of ASTM G173-03, W/(m2·nm)
reflected emissive power of the surface, W/m2
spectral emissive power given by the solar simulator, W/(m2·nm)
the number of the discrete directions
j the j th discrete direction

ce:section.ce:para: Photothermal conversion is a simple and effective method in solar energy utilization [1]. The collector is one of the key components in photothermal conversion process and its performance determines the overall efficiency of solar energy utilization. Conventional solar collectors rely on absorbent plates for photothermal conversion and then transfer the generated heat to the working fluids, resulting in significant heat losses [2] and low solar collection efficiency. Therefore, it is crucial to develop a new solar collector to reduce the heat loss [3].

Recently, the direct absorption solar collector (DASC) is attracting more and more attention due to its high solar collection efficiency. In DASC, the nanofluids act as both photothermal and heat transfer working fluids, omitting the heat transfer process from the absorbent plate to the working fluids and reducing the heat loss significantly. The performance of DASC depends mainly on the optical absorption capacity of the working nanofluids [4,5]. The addition of nanoparticles could enhance the thermophysical and optical absorption properties of fluids [6]. Common nanomaterials include metals [7,8], metal oxides [9–11], MXene [12], and graphene oxide [13]. However, the absorption capacity of nanofluids is proportional to the concentration, while excessive concentrations might result in dispersion instability and agglomeration of nanoparticles. How to balance the optical absorption property and stability still remains challenging.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a quasi-0 dimension carbon-based material with abundant surface functional groups and high photothermal conversion property. The size of CQDs is normally smaller than 10 nm [14]. The principle of their photothermal conversion is that, when exposed to light, CQDs undergo radiative relaxation and non-radiative emission processes, leading to the emission of photons and heat generation, respectively. These emitted photons are subsequently absorbed by other particles within the system, thereby repeating the above process [15]. Compared to conventional nanofluids, CQDs nanofluids demonstrate superior dispersion stability, making them attractive for DASC. Seifikar et al. [16] prepared CQDs nanofluids by microwave heating of polyethylene glycol 200. The CQDs nanofluids could maintain long-term durability in consecutive heating/cooling cycles. Joseph et al. [17] synthesized C-dot/water nanofluids by a one-pot method. The new C-dot/water nanofluids were stabilized for more than 6 months and exhibited corrosion resistance. Lin et al. [18] reported a graphene quantum dot-ethylene glycol (GQD-EG) nanofluids that could be used for medium-temperature DASC. The GQD-EG nanofluids remained well-dispersed after continuously heating at 180 °C for 7 days.

However, the optical absorption range of current CQDs is primarily confined to the ultraviolet region, resulting in the visible and near-infrared regions of sunlight being underutilized. The absorption capacity of CQDs is controlled by the band gap. The smaller band gap would lower the energy of corresponding optical transitions, which in turn improves the absorption capacity of CQDs [19]. To enhance the optical absorption of CQDs in visible and near-infrared regions, an effective strategy is to expand the sp2 structural domains in the carbon core of CQDs or to introduce doping atoms, such as nitrogen (N) and sulfur (S) [20]. The N atom, which is similar to the carbon atom in atomic size, has been widely applied to modulate the optical property of CQDs. The doping of N, especially graphite N, significantly reduces the band gap by injecting excess electrons into the unoccupied π∗ orbitals [21,22]. Fluorine (F) is an element with high electronegativity. The addition of NH4F during the synthesis process induced a rearrangement of the charge distribution of the N atom in the CQDs, thereby influencing the types of N [23].

In view of low optical absorption capacity of current CQDs in visible and near-infrared regions and poor stability, this study developed a novel nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (FPNCQDs) using NH4F and perylene-3, 4, 9, 10-tetracarboxylic dianhydride (PTCDA). The polyene derivatives with large conjugated structures were used as precursors to extend the conjugated sp2 domain, while NH4F was added to increase the graphitic N content. Then, the FPNCQDs was well dispersed into ethylene glycol to prepare the FPNCQDs/ethylene glycol (FPNCQDs/EG) nanofluids. The test results indicated that the newly-fabricated FPNCQDs/EG nanofluids exhibited outstanding absorption performance and high dispersion stability. Furthermore, a theoretical model for the photothermal conversion process of the nanofluids was developed to investigate the effect to carbon quantum dots on the solar collection performance.

Citric acid (CA, 99.5 %), Ammonium fluoride (NH4F, 98 %), and Urea (CH4N2O, 99 %) were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd. Perylene-3, 4, 9, 10-tetracarboxylic dianhydride (PTCDA, 98 %) and Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO, 99.8 %) were provided by Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Ethanol (EA, 99.7 %) and ethylene glycol (EG, 99.5 %) were provided by Shanghai Richjoint Chemical Reagents Co., Ltd. All of the chemicals were used as received.

FPNCQDs were synthesized by the solvothermal method. First, CA (1 g), PTCDA (0.3 g), Urea (4 g), and NH4F (0.4 g) were dissolved in 20 mL of DMSO. The mixed solution was then transferred to a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated in an oven at 160 °C for 5 h. Second, the reaction solution was centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 5 min and the upper liquid layer was then filtered through filter paper to remove large particles. Third, the centrifugation procedure was repeated three times with ethanol, followed by drying to obtain FPNCQDs powder. For comparison, N-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) were synthesized by the same procedure, except without the addition of NH4F and PTCDA.

The obtained FPNCQDs and NCQDs were dispersed into EG and then ultrasonicated for 30 min, respectively, to fabricate the FPNCQDs/EG and NCQDs/EG nanofluids with different concentrations (10 ppm, 20 ppm, 50 ppm, 75 ppm, and 100 ppm).

The morphology and size of FPNCQDs and NCQDs were studied using transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2100, JEOL, Japan). The structures of CQDs were analyzed by Raman spectra (HJY LabRAM Aramis, HORIBA, French). The chemical composition and structure of the FPNCQDs and NCQDs were determined by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Axis Ultra DLD, Kratos, UK) and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, TENSOR, BRUKER, Germany). The transmission spectra of nanofluids in the wavelength range of 300 nm–1500 nm were investigated using an ultraviolet–visible near-infrared spectrophotometer (UV–vis–NIR, Lambda-1050, PerkinElmer, USA).

To assess the photothermal conversion capability of the nanofluids, an experimental setup was developed, as depicted in Fig. 1 . The experimental setup consisted of a solar simulator (Zhongke Micro energy, CME-sol 8100), a photothermal conversion tank, and a data collection system. The specifications of the experimental system were given in Table 1 . In this experiment, a solar simulator was used to maintain stable irradiance. The solar simulator's emissive power was set as 1000 W/m2. The newly-fabricated nanofluids were placed in a cylindrical polypropylene (PP) photothermal conversion tank with the height of 2 cm and inner diameter of 6.2 cm. A polyurethane sponge was wrapped around the photothermal conversion tank to minimize the heat loss. The tank was covered with a piece of quartz glass with high transmittance to prevent evaporation of the nanofluids. Two thermocouples were fixed along the height in the photothermal conversion tank to measure the temperature of the nanofluids.
Fig. 2 showed the theoretical model of DASC. Due to symmetry, the DASC was considered as a two-dimension model.

For the energy balance equation of the DASC, the impacts of incident radiation should be taken into account as an internal heat source. Thus, the energy equation can be written as:
(1)
ρ nf
c pnf
∂ T
∂ t
= −
Q r
+
k nf
(
∂ 2
T
∂
x 2
+
∂ 2
T
∂
y 2
)
(2)
Q r
=
∂
q r
∂ y
where ρ nf is the density of nanofluids, kg/m3, c pnf and k nf are the specific heat, J/(kg·K), and thermal conductivity, W/(m·K), of nanofluids, respectively. q r is the radiative heat flux, W/m2, and T is the temperature, K.

As shown in Fig. 2, the left, right, and bottom surfaces of the DASC were set as the adiabatic boundaries. The upper surface collected incident solar radiation while exchanging heat with the environment by thermal convection. The upper boundary conditions for the heat transfer model were:
(3)
k nf
∂ T
∂ y
|
y = upper
+
E
c , loss
= 0
(4)
E
c , loss
= h
(
T
|
y = upper
−
T amb
)
where E c, loss is the convective heat losses, W/m2, T amb is the average ambient temperature, K, and h is the convective heat transfer coefficient between the DASC and environment, W/(m2·K).

The energy equation of Eq. (1) contained an internal heat source generated by the incident radiation. The spectral attenuation of radiation within the semitransparent nanofluids depth was usually described by the radiative transfer equation (RTE) in participating media [24]. The general form of RTE was:
(5)
Ω ⋅ ∇ I
( Ω )
=
κ nf
I b
( T )
−
β nf
I
( Ω )
+
σ snf
4 π
∫ 0
4 π
I
(
Ω ′
)
⋅ ϕ
(
Ω ' , Ω
)
d Ω '
where I(Ω) is the radiative intensity at a given position following the Ω direction, W/(m2·sr), κ nf, β nf and σ
snf are the absorption, extinction and scattering coefficients of the nanofluids respectively, m−1. I b(T) is the blackbody radiative intensity, W/(m2·sr), and
ϕ
(Ω′, Ω) is the scattering phase function.

Fig. 2 showed that the incident radiation of the solar simulator was only added at the upper surface of the DASC. Thus, the upper surface could be viewed as a transparent wall and the boundary condition was defined as:
(6)
I upper
=
η g
I 0
π
, n ⋅
S j
≤ 0
(7)
I 0
=
∫ 0 ∞
I solar
( λ )
d λ
where η g is the transmittance of the glass cover, %, n is the outward normal vector, j is the number of the discrete directions, S
j is the j th discrete direction, and I solar(λ) is the spectral emissive power given by the solar simulator, W/(m2·nm). For the bottom surface, it could be viewed as opaque wall with the property of diffuse reflection and the boundary conditions, which could be expressed as:
(8)
I bottom
=
χ plate
I b
( T )
+
(
1 −
χ plate
)
I reflected
π
, n ⋅
S j
≤ 0
(9)
I reflected
=
∑
n ⋅
S j
< 0
ω j
I j
|
n ⋅
S j
|
where χ plate is the absorption coefficient of surface and I reflected is the reflected emissive power of the surface, W/m2. In this study, χplate was set as 0.2. For the upper surface of the glass cover and other surfaces of the PP plate, conjugate boundary conditions were assumed.

The DASC model was constructed by integrating the RTE and energy balance equations. In combination with the corresponding boundary conditions, the discrete ordinates method and the finite element method were used to solve the RTE and energy balance equations by COMSOL. The discretization of the computational domain was conducted by the built-in unstructured meshing algorithm of COMSOL. The exact calculation process was described in Ref. [25].

Fig. 3 presented the morphology of the FPNCQDs and NCQDs. The FPNCQDs exhibited a spherical shape with a size distribution ranging from 1.0 nm to 5.0 nm and an average diameter of 3.11 nm. However, the average diameter of the NCQDs was just 2.37 nm. The results showed that the addition of PTCDA and NH4F increased the size of NCQDs, which could lead to the expansion of the sp2 conjugated domains. The HRTEM images showed identical high-resolution lattice fringes for both CQDs, with a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (1 0 0) plane of graphitic carbon [26].

To analyze the structure of FPNCQDs and NCQDs, FTIR spectra and Raman spectroscopy analysis were performed, as shown in Fig. 4 . In Fig. 4(a), the peaks at 3351 cm−1 and 3164 cm−1 in both FPNCQDs and NCQDs corresponded to the vibrations of O-H and N-H bonds, respectively. The stability of CQDs in fluids was attributed to the hydrogen bonding and electrostatic coupling between the CQDs and EG Ref. [27]. The peak at 1644.8 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching modes of C=O/C=N for carboxyl. Additionally, both CQDs showed that the peak at 1290 cm−1 for the stretching vibrations of C-O/C-N bonds. Moreover, the peaks at 1021 cm−1 and 941 cm−1 in PTCDA and FPNCQDs corresponded to the C-O-C bond [28], indicating the successful integration of PTCDA into FPNCQDs. In Fig. 4(b), Raman spectra was used to evaluate the degree of graphitization of FPNCQDs and NCQDs. Notably, the broad D and G bands were observed at 1350 and 1560 cm−1, respectively. The G band related to the vibration of sp2 bonded carbon and the D band was attributed to the disordered carbon domain caused by defects [29]. The higher intensity ratio (I D/I G) of FPNCQDs compared to NCQDs was due to the addition of NH4F during their synthesis, which increased the number of defects [23].

The chemical compositions and valence states of FPNCQDs and NCQDs were analyzed through XPS. Fig. 5 (a) confirmed the successful doping of N in both CQDs. In Fig. 5(b), a strong peak at 284.8 eV was observed, corresponding to the C-C/C=C bonds of the sp2 conjugate structure. The peak area of C-C/C=C bonds suggested that FPNCQDs exhibited higher sp2 C content than NCQDs, indicating that the addition of PTCDA during its synthesis process contributed to the formation of the sp2 conjugated structure. Additionally, peaks of C-N (285.65 eV), C-O (286.95 eV) and C=O (288.71 eV) were also observed [20]. The N 1s spectra of FPNCQDs, as shown in Fig. 5 (c), demonstrated three peaks at 399.45 eV, 400.11 eV, and 400.79 eV, which were attributed to pyridine N, amino N, and graphite N, respectively [30]. However, compared to the N 1s spectrum of NCQDs, all three N peaks of FPNCQDs were shifted towards lower binding energies, due to the increase in the negative charge of N. This shift may originate from the increased content of off-domain π electrons in the system after graphite N doping [31]. The calculations showed that the graphite N content in FPNCQDs was 3.92 %, which was higher than that of NCQDs (1.46 %). The results indicated that NH4F contributed to the formation of graphite N during the synthesis process. In the O 1s spectrum, two peaks corresponding to C-O (533.46 eV) and C=O bonds (531.83 eV) were observed [32].

Solar absorption capability is crucial for effective photothermal conversion, which is usually characterized by transmittance and extinction coefficient. Transmittance is defined as the ratio of the transmitted light flux to the incident light flux. The extinction coefficient is derived from the transmittance following Beer-Lambert's law. Generally, a lower transmittance and a higher extinction coefficient indicate superior optical absorption performance of nanofluids. The transmittance spectrum and extinction coefficient of FPNCQDs/EG and NCQDs/EG nanofluids at a concentration of 100 ppm were obtained from Fig. 6 . In Fig. 6(a), the addition of CQDs significantly reduced the transmittance of the base fluids EG. Fig. 6(b) illustrated that the extinction coefficient of FPNCQDs/EG nanofluids in the visible region (400–760 nm) was considerably higher than that of NCQDs/EG nanofluids, approximately 72.8 % higher. To directly evaluate the ability of the nanofluids to absorb solar radiation, the spectral irradiance of the nanofluids was calculated based on the transmittance, as shown in Fig. 7 . The solar spectrum is the AM 1.5 solar irradiance spectrum according to ASTM G173-03. In Fig. 7, FPNCQDs/EG and NCQDs/EG nanofluids absorbed almost all the solar radiation in the 300–700 nm range whereas EG only absorbed 9.9 % of solar radiation in the same wavelength range. In the near-infrared band (760–1100 nm), FPNCQDs/EG nanofluids exhibited a 38.4 % increase in spectral irradiance compared to NCQDs/EG nanofluids. These results clearly demonstrated FPNCQDs/EG nanofluids achieved much superior absorption performance, especially in visible and near-infrared wavelengths. The enhanced absorption performance is due to the expansion of the conjugated sp2 structural domains and the increased graphite N content in FPNCQDs. Compared to pyridine N and amine N, graphite N narrowed the band gap, which enhanced the light absorption [33].

The band gap energy determines the ability of CQDs to absorb the light at different wavelengths. In general, the narrower the band gap, the greater the light absorption ability. The band gap energy of CQDs can be calculated using the Tauc plot method, with the specific formula as follows:
(10)
κ h ν = B ⋅
(
h ν −
E g
)
1 / 2
(11)
A = κ l C
(12)
κ =
A
l C
=
A k
(13)
(
A h ν
k
)
2
= h ν −
E g
where κ is the absorption coefficient, m−1, h is Planck constant, J·s, v is the incident photon frequency, Hz, B is the proportionality constant, A is the absorbance, l is the optical range length (cm), and C is the concentration of nanofluids (ppm). In the calculation lC can be considered as a constant k. From Eq. (13), (Ahv)2 is linearly related to hv. Consequently, the graph can be tangentially obtained to get E g by taking hv as the abscissa and (Ahv)2 as the ordinate. As shown in Fig. 8 , FPNCQDs had a narrower band gap. This narrower band gap resulted in stronger absorbance, which was consistent with the previous results.

Given that the concentration is closely related to the optical absorption ability, the transmission spectra and spectral irradiance of different concentrations of FPNCQDs/EG nanofluids were tested, as illustrated in Fig. 9 . Overall, the transmittance of the nanofluids decreased notably by increasing the concentration. Even at the concentration of 20 ppm for FPNCQDs/EG nanofluids, the average transmittance decreased by about 28.35 % compared to that of EG. In Fig. 9, FPNCQDs/EG nanofluids with a concentration of 50 ppm absorbed almost 100 % of solar radiation in the range of 300–700 nm. Further increasing the concentration had no significant effect on the absorption performance in 300–700 nm range, but could increase the absorption performance in 700–1100 nm range.

Dispersion stability is another key parameter for nanofluids in DASC. Normally, the dispersion stability will deteriorate as the concentration increases and the nanofluids at low concentrations tended to be more stable. Thus, the concentration of 20 ppm, 50 ppm, 75 ppm, and 100 ppm were selected for the dispersion stability test. Fig. 10 (a) and (b) illustrated that both nanofluids showed no precipitation after 14 days of storage. Furthermore, the dispersion stability of the nanofluids was accurately assessed by measuring the change in transmittance of the two nanofluids over a 14-day period, as shown in Fig. 10(c) and (d). The results showed that the transmittance of FPNCQDs/EG nanofluids remained almost unchanged after 14 days of storage, while the transmittance of NCQDs/EG nanofluids just decreased by 8.47 % in the same period. These findings suggested that FPNCQDs/EG nanofluids achieved superior dispersion stability to NCQDs/EG nanofluids.

To further assess the stability of FPNCQDs/EG nanofluids, the cycling performance was evaluated by repeating the solar heating and natural cooling process. The temperature and temperature difference between H1 and H2 were displayed in Fig. 11
. Here, H1 and H2 represented the heights of two thermocouples located at 0.6 cm and 1.3 cm below the upper surface of the nanofluids, respectively. It could be observed that the FPNCQDs/EG nanofluids maintained a high heat collection performance, with the maximum temperature fluctuations not exceeding 6 %. This indicated that the FPNCQDs/EG nanofluids exhibited high stability and was a promising working fluid for DASC.

Photothermal conversion experiments were conducted to evaluate the heat collection performance of two nanofluids. Two thermocouples were fixed in the photothermal conversion tank to measure the variation of the temperature at different positions during the photothermal conversion process. H1 and H2 represented the heights of two thermocouples located at 0.6 cm and 1.3 cm below the upper surface of the nanofluids, respectively. Fig. 12 depicted the temperature variations of two nanofluids at a concentration of 20 ppm and EG during exposure to solar radiation for 60 min. The temperature of the FPNCQDs/EG nanofluids at height H1 increased from 24 °C to 67.2 °C in 60 min, representing an 8.8 °C higher temperature rise than that of the EG. The temperature of the NCQDs/EG nanofluids increased by 41 °C during the same period. The average temperature was obtained by averaging the two thermocouples. The temperature of the FPNCQDs/EG nanofluids increased from 24 °C to 65.7 °C in 60 min, which was 2 °C higher than that of NCQDs/EG nanofluids.

The photothermal conversion efficiency of the nanofluids is closely related to concentrations. Therefore, the average temperature variation of FPNCQDs/EG nanofluids at various concentrations was examined for 60 min of irradiation, as depicted in Fig. 13 . The concentrations ranged from 10 ppm to 100 ppm. The average temperatures were obtained by the two thermocouples. At 10 ppm, the temperature of FPNCQDs/EG nanofluids rose from 28.3 °C to 65.2 °C. The temperature rise of the FPNCQDs/EG nanofluids increased as the concentration increased to 50 ppm. However, with further increases in concentration, the temperature rise was minimal and even decreased.

To further investigate the temperature distribution in DASC at different concentrations of nanofluids, a mathematical model was developed. Before the formal simulation, the mesh-independence test and model validation were performed. Table 2 presents the mesh-independence test for the DASC simulation. The concentration of the nanofluids was fixed as 50 ppm. Three kinds of mesh, including 400, 503, and 769 were used and the temperature of the nanofluid after 1-h illumination at the test point was obtained. Once the mesh number approached 503, the relative deviation between the calculated and experimental values was reduced to 0.028 %. Thus, Mesh II was used in the following simulations. What's more, to fully validate the mathematical model for the whole system, concentrations of 10 ppm, 50 ppm, and 100 ppm of FPNCQDs/EG nanofluids were selected for comparison, as shown in Fig. 14 . The largest relative deviation between the numerical and experimental results was within 4.5 %, validating the theoretical model.

In Fig. 15 (a), when EG was used as the working fluid, the irradiation was mainly absorbed by the PP plate. It was mainly because the extinction coefficient of EG was too small, making it difficult for the radiation to be absorbed by EG. Thus, the areas with high temperature in EG condition were mainly concentrated on the bottom surface of the DASC. For the nanofluids at 10 ppm, the high temperature region was observed to shift towards the middle region of the DASC. Further increase in the concentration could enhance the photothermal conversion performance and the solar radiation was mainly absorbed by the upper nanofluids, as illustrated in Fig. 15(c) and (d). It can be concluded that at low concentrations, all the nanofluids could be heated by solar radiation. However, at higher concentrations, only the upper nanofluids was illuminated by the solar radiation, which resulted in the much higher surface temperature and inevitable heat loss at the surface. Therefore, the optimized height of the solar collector should be determined based on the concentration (light absorption ability), thermal properties, including specific heat and thermal conductivity, illumination intensity, surrounding temperature, cost and the target for solar collection.

To evaluate the overall solar collection performance, the average solar collection efficiency of the DASC was defined as:
(14)
η =
m
c pnf
Δ T
I A t
where m is the mass of the nanofluids, kg, and c pnf the specific heat, J/(kg·K) of the nanofluids. I is the emissive power of the solar simulator, W/m2, A is the solar collector area of the DASC, m2, and ΔT is the average total temperature difference of the DASC during the test time. The average solar collection efficiency for FPNCQDs/EG nanofluids with different concentrations was calculated, as shown in Fig. 16 . As the concentration increased, the average efficiency exhibited a rising and then declining trend.
The nanofluids at 10 ppm demonstrated the highest solar collection efficiency considering both photothermal conversion and heat loss from the surface.

Considering the low optical absorption capacity of current CQDs in visible and near-infrared regions and poor stability, this paper developed a novel FPNCQDs/EG nanofluids with excellent heat collecting ability and strong stability using NH4F and perylene-3, 4, 9, 10-tetracarboxylic dianhydride (PTCDA). The photothermal conversion property, especially in visible and near-infrared regions, was significantly improved. The polyene derivative with large conjugated structures was used as precursors to extend the conjugated sp2 domain, and NH4F to increase the graphitic N content, which resulted in a reduction in the band gap of FPNCQDs, and enhance the optical absorption capacity. Moreover, FPNCQDs/EG nanofluids exhibited superior stability, with no precipitation and negligible alteration in transmittance even after being stored for 14 days and cyclic test. The high dispersion stability of FPNCQDs/EG nanofluids was mainly attributed to FPNCQDs interacting with EG through hydrogen bonding and electrostatic coupling. Finally, a theoretical model for the photothermal conversion process of the nanofluids was developed to investigate the effect to carbon quantum dots on the solar collection performance. The simulation results indicated that the FPNCQDs/EG nanofluids at 10 ppm demonstrated the highest solar collection efficiency considering both photothermal conversion and heat loss from the surface.

Yuxin Liu: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Chuanshuai Dong: Writing – original draft, Investigation. Chaohua Peng: Software. Tao Zhang: Investigation, Formal analysis. Lizhi Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51936005 and No. 52476072).
